skill.name: accessibility
skill.description: Audit and improve web accessibility using WCAG 2.2 guidance, accessible code patterns, and manual testing checklists.
skill.summary: Review web interfaces for WCAG 2.2 accessibility issues and apply practical remediation patterns.
skill.author: addyosmani

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, filesystem, sensitive data, weak crypto, and network patterns. Review found Markdown code fences, accessibility examples, reference links, and optional user-run audit commands, not automatic execution or malicious behavior. No prompt injection, credential access, exfiltration, or hidden runtime logic was found.

low_findings.title: Optional Accessibility CLI Commands
low_findings.description: The skill documents Lighthouse and axe CLI commands for accessibility testing. They are not executed by the skill and require explicit user action, but they can contact a target URL or install packages if copied and run.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct shell commands are present in a Markdown testing checklist. Confidence is high that they are documentation only, but they still represent optional external command guidance.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The skill links to public WCAG, ARIA, Deque, and accessibility tool resources. These are static documentation links and do not transmit user data or run automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible Markdown references to reputable accessibility resources. No code path sends data to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Static Alerts From Markdown Syntax and Examples
low_findings.description: Most static alerts come from Markdown code fences, inline backticks, relative documentation links, password field examples, and WCAG numbering. They are not executable Ruby backticks, credential reads, path traversal, or weak cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown prose, tables, links, and example markup. No executable skill hook, filesystem operation, cryptographic operation, or credential access is present.

content.value_statement: Many web interfaces fail accessibility checks and exclude users with disabilities. This skill guides audits and fixes using WCAG 2.2, code patterns, and testing checklists.
actual_capabilities: Explains WCAG 2.2 principles, levels, and common success criteria.
Provides accessible patterns for forms, modals, tabs, skip links, and live regions.
Guides checks for keyboard access, focus visibility, contrast, labels, and target size.
Includes manual testing steps for screen readers, zoom, reduced motion, and focus order.
References automated testing with Lighthouse and axe for accessibility audits.
limitations: Does not run accessibility scans by itself.
Does not replace testing with disabled users or assistive technology experts.
Does not guarantee legal compliance in any jurisdiction.
Examples may need adaptation for each framework and design system.

use_cases.title: Fix a Frontend Feature
use_cases.description: Review a component for labels, focus behavior, keyboard access, ARIA usage, and WCAG 2.2 issues before release.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Compliance Review
use_cases.description: Create a prioritized accessibility checklist for a product area using WCAG levels and common impact categories.

use_cases.title: Improve Design QA
use_cases.description: Check flows for contrast, target size, visible focus, motion preferences, and accessible alternatives for non-text content.

prompt_templates.title: Review One Page
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner audit of a single screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this page for basic accessibility issues. Focus on alt text, labels, headings, contrast, keyboard access, and focus indicators.

prompt_templates.title: Fix a Component
prompt_templates.scenario: Practical remediation for a UI component
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this component against WCAG 2.2 AA. Suggest specific fixes for ARIA, keyboard behavior, focus management, and visible labels.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Release Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an accessibility release checklist for this feature. Include automated checks, manual keyboard tests, screen reader checks, and acceptance criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Accessibility Remediation Sprint
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prioritization across a product area
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these accessibility findings and group them by severity, WCAG criterion, affected users, implementation effort, and recommended sprint priority.

output_examples.input: A modal opens, but Tab moves behind it and Escape does nothing.
output: Issue: Keyboard focus is not contained within the dialog.
Impact: Keyboard and screen reader users can lose context behind the modal.
Fix: Move focus into the dialog, cycle Tab within focusable controls, and close on Escape.

output_examples.input: A signup form uses placeholder text instead of labels.
output: Issue: Inputs lack persistent programmatic labels.
Impact: Screen reader users may not understand each field after typing starts.
Fix: Add visible labels and connect help text with described-by relationships.

output_examples.input: A dashboard uses color alone to show error and success states.
output: Issue: Status depends only on color perception.
Impact: Users with color vision differences may miss important states.
Fix: Add text, icons with accessible names, and sufficient non-text contrast.

best_practices: Test keyboard navigation before adding custom ARIA patterns.
Prefer native HTML controls before building custom interactive elements.
Combine automated checks with manual screen reader and zoom testing.
anti_patterns: Do not remove focus outlines without providing a visible replacement.
Do not use placeholder text as the only form label.
Do not rely on color alone to communicate errors or status.

faq.question: Does this skill run accessibility tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and example commands. A user must run any external tool deliberately.

faq.question: Which standard does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on WCAG 2.2, especially practical Level A and Level AA checks.

faq.question: Can it help with screen reader support?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for labels, live regions, focus order, and screen reader testing.

faq.question: Can it guarantee legal compliance?
faq.answer: No. It supports accessibility remediation, but legal review depends on jurisdiction and product context.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What should teams test manually?
faq.answer: Teams should test keyboard access, focus order, zoom, high contrast, reduced motion, and screen reader behavior.
